title-group.article-title: Environmental impacts of metro rail construction in chennai on air quality noise pollution and urban sustainability

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/056ep7w45grid.412612.20000 0004 1760 9349Centre for Environment and Climate, Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan (Deemed to be University), Bhubaneswar, India

3Department of Civil Engineering, Ramco Institute of Technology, Rajapalayam, India
4https://ror.org/00gbrgx34grid.464974.c0000 0004 1775 7296School of Energy and Environment, NICMAR University, Pune, Maharashtra 411045 India

You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Metro rail construction can introduce localized environmental disturbances during the development phase despite its long-term benefits for sustainable urban mobility. This study evaluates the air and noise pollution impacts of ongoing Chennai Metro Rail construction across Corridors 3 and 4, covering pre-monsoon, monsoon, and post-monsoon seasons. Air-quality monitoring was conducted for key pollutants—PM2.5, PM10, SO2, NO2, and CO—using high-volume air samplers and portable analyzers, while noise levels were continuously recorded using digital sound level meters. Spatial mapping with GIS illustrated pollutant dispersion and identified localized hotspots of high particulate and acoustic loads. Results indicated pronounced seasonal and spatial variability in particulate matter concentrations across construction corridors, with PM10 levels peaking during the pre-monsoon period. Although average particulate concentrations largely remained within National Ambient Air Quality Standards, statistically significant seasonal differences were observed. Noise levels frequently approached or exceeded CPCB limits, particularly near densely built commercial corridors. Correlation analysis revealed a significant positive association between PM2.5 and PM10, indicating common emission sources primarily related to construction activities and associated vehicular traffic. Health surveys indicated that nearly half of the nearby residents reported dizziness and other discomforts, along with respiratory and ocular irritation, which were spatially associated with areas of elevated air and noise levels. The findings indicate that metro construction can generate measurable localized environmental and public health stress, yet these impacts are temporary and can be mitigated through dust suppression, acoustic barriers, and controlled scheduling. Integrating real-time monitoring, community engagement, and adaptive environmental management can transform metro projects into resilient pathways toward sustainable urban development.

funding-source.institution: Siksha 'O' Anusandhan Deemed To Be University

open-access.p: Open access funding provided by Siksha 'O' Anusandhan (Deemed To Be University)

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Metro systems have been successfully introduced in crowded cities worldwide over the past few decades, and are increasingly preferred modes of transport, especially in congested cities during peak traffic hours1–3. The growing environmental and socio-economic implications associated with such infrastructure projects has led to extensive assessment studies, globally4–8.

Multiple Indian cities have documented environmental impacts associated with metro construction. For instance, in Delhi, PM2.5 and PM10 levels rose significantly due to excavation and increased vehicle movement9. Even with control strategies, particulate levels remained high10. Elevated noise levels near residential areas were reported due to piling and heavy equipment11,12. In Mumbai, traffic diversions and prolonged idling near construction zones led to increased NO2 and CO levels13,14. Noise pollution was also recorded beyond permissible limits in urban zones15. Internationally, metro projects such as Athens Metro Line 3 faced similar environmental consequences7. In Kolkata, vehicular emissions and poor dust control during metro construction affected urban air quality16. Short-term spikes in PM2.5, PM10, and NOx were noted in Mumbai17,18, while in Pune, noise impacts led to the adoption of mitigation strategies like sound barriers and restricted working hours19,20. Bangalore also documented elevated PM levels and recommended wet suppression and scheduling adjustments21. While short-term environmental disturbances are evident during construction, the long-term operational benefits of metro systems—including reduced vehicular emissions and improved urban mobility—can substantially offset these initial environmental costs22.

While environmental assessments exist for metro construction in other Indian cities, there is a paucity of literature providing seasonally stratified and site-specific analyses of both air and noise pollution at active metro rail construction zones. Besides, the unique urban morphology, monsoonal climate, and regulatory environment of Chennai city present a different scenario, compared to cities like Delhi and Mumbai. In fact, reported prior studies seemed to have not adequately explored seasonal variations (esp. particularly monsoon rains), influence particulate dispersion and dynamics of gaseous pollutant (e.g., SO2, NO2) during ongoing construction activities. Besides, spatial variability in noise levels and the effectiveness of mitigation strategies in densely built-up areas of Chennai have also not been comprehensively assessed in prior studies.

The present study addresses this critical gap by monitoring pollution levels across pre-monsoon, monsoon, and post-monsoon periods, capturing the temporal and spatial dynamics of pollutant dispersion and accumulation. The primary objective of this study is to evaluate the cumulative environmental impact of ongoing metro rail construction in Chennai by quantifying key air pollutants (PM2.5, PM10, SO2, NO2, and CO) and noise levels across major construction corridors. The findings are expected to provide evidence-based insights to inform more effective environmental mitigation policies and help develop location- and season-specific guidelines for managing urban construction pollution in South Indian metropolitan contexts.

sec.title: Study area
sec.p: The monitoring of air and noise pollution was conducted across three distinct seasonal phases—pre-monsoon, monsoon, and post-monsoon—to capture seasonal variability in environmental parameters. The selected locations for monitoring are presented in Fig. 1, which illustrates the geographical spread of the study area across various metro construction sites in Chennai.

The sampling locations included: 3 A-Ayanavaram, 3B-Otteri, 3 C-Pattalam, 3D-Kellys, 3E-Purasawakkam, 3 F-Perambur Baracks Road (Lakshmi Mahal), 3G-Thoraipakkam, 3 H-Mettukuppam, 3I-PTC Colony, 3 J-Okkiyampet, 3 K-Karapakkam, 3 L-Okkiyam Thoraipakkam, 3 M-Okkiyam Maduvu, 4 A-NH4 Bypass & Poonamallee Flyover Junction, 4B-Kumunan Chavadi Bus Stop (MSS Nagar), 5 C-Alwarthiru Nagar Junction, 4D-Permal Street (Shradha Nagar), 4E-Vadapalani Junction, 4 F-Kodambakkam Meenakshi College, and 4G-Santhome Church.

The study focused on Corridor 3 (Madhavaram–SIPCOT), and Corridor 4 (Light House–Poonamallee), key segments under Phase 2 of the Chennai Metro Rail Project. These corridors were selected based on their high construction activity levels and the environmental sensitivity of the surrounding areas. They traverse densely populated, traffic-heavy zones, including residential, commercial, and mixed-use neighborhoods. For example, Corridor 3 includes areas like Ayanavaram and Purasawakkam, which experience significant traffic congestion and are likely to be impacted by emissions and construction-related disturbances.

caption.p: Study area showing metro construction corridors and air and noise monitoring locations in Chennai. Map generated by the authors using ArcGIS 10.8 (Esri Inc., Redlands, CA, USA; https://www.esri.com/arcgis) based on GPS-referenced field survey data.

sec.p: Each corridor was chosen to reflect a range of urban environmental conditions, including proximity to industrial hubs, residential colonies, arterial roads, and ecologically sensitive sites. This strategic selection enables a nuanced assessment of how metro construction affects environmental quality across diverse settings. The variation in land use and population density among these sites also allows for the analysis of site-specific pollution patterns, providing deeper insight into how construction activities influence public exposure to air and noise pollutants. These insights are crucial for informing targeted mitigation strategies and improving sustainable practices in large-scale infrastructure projects in urban India.

sec.title: Monitoring equipment and sampling protocols
sec.p: Air quality assessment was carried out during the pre-monsoon, monsoon, and post-monsoon seasons using standardized procedures. Each sampling event lasted 24 h, and monitoring was conducted at multiple stations selected based on traffic density and urban activity.

sec.title: Pre-monsoon and monsoon sampling
sec.p: A High-Volume Air Sampler (HVAS) was employed for monitoring Total Suspended Particulates (TSP), PM10, PM2.5, SO2, NO2, and CO. The sampler operated for 24 h, drawing approximately 1500 m3 of ambient air through pre-weighed glass fiber filters, which were reweighed post-sampling to determine particulate mass concentration23,24. Gaseous pollutants were collected using absorption tubes connected to the HVAS. The collected air samples were subsequently analyzed using standard titrimetric methods to quantify SO2, NO2, and CO concentrations, following established procedures25.

sec.title: Area stratification and station setup
sec.p: The study area was segmented into four distinct bands based on traffic-related parameters including vehicle speed, volume, and composition. Monitoring stations were established within each zone. Identical sampling protocols and instruments were maintained across all stations to ensure comparability26,27.

sec.title: Post-monsoon sampling
sec.p: During the post-monsoon phase, air quality monitoring was conducted using portable air quality detectors. These devices provided real-time measurement of PM10 and PM2.5, based on laser-scattering detection, while SO2, NO2, and CO concentrations were measured using portable gas analyzers operating on non-dispersive infrared (NDIR) technology28.
The concentration of air pollutants obtained through these studies (namely, PM2.5, PM10, SO2, NO2, and CO) are compared to CPCB NAAQS Limit (Table 1).

caption.p: National ambient air quality standards (NAAQS) prescribed by CPCB (India).

table-wrap-foot.p: Source: Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB). (2019). National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS). Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change, Government of India.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Descriptive statistics were used to summarize seasonal and corridor-wise variations in air pollutant concentrations. Pearson’s correlation analysis was performed to examine the relationship between PM2.5 and PM10 concentrations across corridors and seasons. Seasonal differences in particulate matter concentrations were assessed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), with season considered as the grouping factor. Statistical significance was evaluated at the 95% confidence level (p < 0.05).

sec.title: Environmental noise monitoring
sec.p: Noise levels were measured using a digital sound level meter equipped with a data logger. Measurements were conducted for 24 h at each station, recording both Leq_day and Leq_night values. The data were subsequently compared with national noise standards to assess compliance29.

Noise data were analyzed using descriptive statistics, including average and maximum equivalent sound levels (Leq_day and Leq_night), and compared against Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)30 ambient noise standards for relevant land-use categories. Inferential statistical testing was not applied to noise data due to the dominant influence of operational and traffic-related factors on temporal variability.

sec.title: Geospatial mapping of the air quality parameters
sec.p: Air quality parameters, namely PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, CO, and ambient noise levels, were monitored at designated sites along the metro rail construction corridor and at selected control locations away from construction influence. Sampling points were chosen based on their proximity to excavation areas, traffic diversion routes, and residential zones. The geographic coordinates of each site were recorded using handheld GPS equipment to enable spatial referencing and mapping.

All pollutant concentration data were compiled, quality-checked, and processed for geospatial analysis using ArcGIS 10.8 (Esri Inc., Redlands, CA, USA). Spatial interpolation was carried out to visualize the dispersion of pollutants and to identify zones of elevated concentrations. Buffer and overlay analyses were performed to relate pollutant distribution patterns with surrounding land use, road networks, and population density. Thematic maps were generated for each parameter corresponding to the pre-construction, construction, and operational phases, enabling phase-wise comparison of concentration levels.

The resulting maps clearly illustrated spatial gradients and hotspot areas associated with major construction and vehicular activities. These geospatial visualizations provided strong support for interpreting variations discussed in the Results section and aided in establishing spatial correlations between measured pollutant levels and localized construction impacts.

sec.title: Assessment of impact on health of the residents
sec.p: A structured household survey was conducted among residents living within varying distances from the metro construction corridor to assess potential health effects of air and noise pollution. Participants were selected from multiple residential clusters representing near-site (within 500 m), mid-zone (500 m−1 km), and control areas (beyond 2 km). The questionnaire collected details on demographic profile, duration of residence, daily exposure to dust and noise, and self-reported symptoms such as coughing, throat irritation, eye discomfort, headache, fatigue, and respiratory distress.

Survey data were analyzed to determine the frequency and spatial distribution of reported symptoms across the three distance zones. These observations were compared with corresponding air quality and noise measurements obtained from the monitoring program to identify possible associations between exposure levels and health complaints. Emphasis was placed on highlighting responses from vulnerable groups such as children, the elderly, and individuals with pre-existing respiratory conditions. This integrative approach, combining monitored environmental parameters with field survey responses, enabled a practical understanding of how deteriorated air quality and elevated noise during metro rail construction influenced community well-being. The findings formed a basis for the interpretation and policy implications discussed in the subsequent sections, emphasizing the need for effective environmental management and mitigation during large-scale infrastructure development.

sec.title: Variability assessment of air quality parameters across the corridors (with reference to CPCB standards)
sec.p: The box plots (Fig. 2) illustrate the statistical variability of air pollutant concentrations across the study corridors during different construction phases. Higher pollutant concentrations were observed at locations with intensive construction activity esp. PM2.5 and PM10 levels. This aligns with the findings that construction-related activities such as excavation and material transport are primary sources of particulate pollution. The post-monsoon phase shows a slight reduction in pollution levels, which could be attributed to rain-induced dust suppression, although the gaseous pollutants like NO2 and SO2 remain relatively stable, indicating persistent emission sources from combustion activities.

caption.p: Box-plots representing overall variability of air pollutants across the Corridors.

sec.p: Table 2 presents a comparison of observed air pollutant concentrations during metro rail construction with the National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) prescribed by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)30. Although all monitored particulate and gaseous pollutants largely remained within CPCB regulatory limits, distinct seasonal and spatial variability was observed across metro construction corridors. Higher concentrations during the pre-monsoon period reflect intensified dust generation and vehicular activity under dry conditions, while monsoon rainfall significantly suppressed particulate levels.

caption.p: Comparison of observed air pollutant concentrations with CPCB standards.

table-wrap-foot.p: *Exceedance refers to maximum observed concentration exceeding the CPCB 8-hour guideline value for CO at select locations, likely influenced by traffic congestion and construction equipment idling.

sec.title: Distributional and relational characteristics of air quality parameters
sec.p: Figure 3 highlights the interrelationships between particulate and gaseous pollutants across the corridors. The strong positive correlation between PM2.5 and PM10 concentrations suggests that both fine and coarse particulate matter are emitted from similar sources, such as construction activities and vehicular emissions. The weaker relationship between SO2 and other pollutants indicates that sulfur emissions from construction machinery and fuel combustion play a minor role in the overall pollution levels, consistent with the low sulfur content in urban fuels. These relationships are crucial for designing effective mitigation strategies, especially those targeting particulate matter and vehicular emissions.

caption.p: Overall distribution and relationship of air pollutants in all corridors.

sec.p: A statistically significant positive correlation was observed between PM2.5 and PM10 concentrations across corridors and seasons (Pearson’s r = 0.76, R2 = 0.58, p = 0.018). This relationship indicates that fine and coarse particulate matter are influenced by common emission sources, primarily construction activities and associated vehicular movement.

sec.title: Location-specific seasonal variability of air quality parameters (statistical assessment)
sec.p: Figure 4 illustrates the seasonal variation of air quality parameters across different monitoring locations within the metro rail construction corridors. Distinct seasonal trends were observed, influenced by variations in meteorological conditions, intensity of construction activity, and local emission dynamics, thereby indicating that particulate pollution (PM2.5 and PM10) peaks during dry months (summer), when excavation and material handling activities are most intense. The monsoon season, however, brings a significant reduction in particulate pollution due to rainfall, which aids in the settling of dust particles. Gaseous pollutants like NO2 and CO show moderate increases during dry months, likely due to heightened traffic and construction activity. The winter months show a slight rebound in particulate and gaseous pollutants, possibly due to reduced wind and atmospheric dispersion, emphasizing the need for dust suppression during dry periods and emission management.

caption.p: Seasonal variation of air pollutants along specific the corridors.

sec.p: One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) revealed statistically significant seasonal variation in PM2.5 concentrations across the study corridors (F = 11.13, p = 0.0096). PM2.5 levels were highest during the pre-monsoon period and lowest during the monsoon season, indicating effective suppression of fine particulate matter by rainfall and associated meteorological conditions. A similar seasonal trend was observed for PM10, with one-way ANOVA indicating statistically significant differences across seasons (F = 5.83, p = 0.039). Reduced PM10 concentrations during the monsoon period reflect wet deposition and reduced resuspension of coarse particulates during rainfall. Overall, inferential analysis suggests that seasonal factors may significantly modulate particulate concentrations during metro construction, with monsoon conditions exerting a statistically significant suppressive effect on both PM2.5 and PM10.

Gaseous pollutants (SO2, NO2, and CO) exhibited comparatively limited seasonal variability and remained largely within regulatory limits across all monitoring locations, indicating a weaker influence of rainfall on their concentrations. Exploratory statistical testing indicated that seasonal differences in gaseous pollutants were not statistically significant (p > 0.05).

sec.title: Diurnal and seasonal variability of noise levels at the corridors
sec.p: The diurnal and seasonal noise patterns reveal that noise levels are higher during peak construction times, typically in the morning and evening, when traffic and construction activities are most intense (Fig. 5). The monsoon months show lower noise levels, likely due to reduced construction activity and lower vehicular movement during rain. Spatial variability is also noted, with higher noise levels in commercially dense areas compared to residential zones, reflecting the combined impact of construction, traffic, and daily human activity.

caption.p: Diurnal and seasonal variation of noise levels at corridors.

sec.p: Noise levels frequently exceeded CPCB limits during peak construction hours, particularly in commercial and mixed-use zones. Elevated Leq values were observed during daytime excavation and material handling activities, while reduced levels were recorded during monsoon periods due to curtailed construction activity.

sec.title: Spatio-temporal distribution of air quality parameters and relative hotspot patterns
sec.p: The maps (Fig. 6) illustrate the spatio-temporal distribution of key air pollutants—PM2.5, PM10, SO2, NO2, and CO—across the metro construction corridors, highlighting relative concentration hotspots within the study area. These hotspots represent localized elevations in pollutant levels associated with construction activity and traffic density, rather than systematic exceedance of Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) regulatory limits.

The spatial patterns indicate that factors such as traffic volume, construction intensity, and localized meteorological conditions (e.g., coastal dispersion effects in Corridor 3 A) influence pollutant accumulation. Areas in close proximity to active metro stations, excavation zones, and material handling sites exhibited comparatively higher particulate concentrations, particularly during the pre-monsoon period. Corridor-wise comparison suggests that Corridors 3 A and 3B experienced higher particulate loads than Corridor 4, reflecting differences in construction phase and activity intensity.

Overall, the observed spatial heterogeneity underscores the importance of site-specific and phase-based mitigation strategies, even when average pollutant concentrations remain within CPCB standards. The spatio-temporal analysis thus complements the seasonal and statistical findings by identifying locations where targeted dust-control and traffic-management measures would be most effective.

caption.p: Spatial distribution of particulate and gaseous pollutants and noiseintensity across metro construction corridors in Chennai. Maps weregenerated by the authors using ArcGIS 10.8 (Esri, Redlands, CA, USA;https://www.esri.com/arcgis) based on field monitoring data and GPSreferencedsampling locations

sec.title: Health impacts associated with construction activities on the residents along the corridors
sec.p: Figure 7 presents the distribution of self-reported health problems among residents and workers living or operating near different metro construction corridors in Chennai. These observations reflect perceived physiological and psychological discomforts associated with proximity to construction-related environmental stressors, rather than clinically diagnosed health outcomes. To reduce potential confounding from short-term exposure, survey participants included in the analysis had a minimum residence or work duration of one year within the respective corridor influence zones.

Across the study corridors, reported symptoms included eye irritation, breathing difficulties, headaches, insomnia, and what respondents described as “illusion.” In Corridor 3 A, approximately 11.1% of respondents reported eye irritation, 20.2% breathing issues, 24.4% headaches, 4.7% insomnia, and 39.6% reported illusion-like discomfort. Corresponding values for Corridor 3B were 14% (eye irritation), 10.1% (breathing issues), 15.8% (headaches), 10.2% (insomnia), and 49.8% (illusion). In Corridor 4, 13.6% reported eye irritation, 16.9% breathing issues, 23.5% headaches, 6.2% insomnia, and 39.8% reported illusion-like symptoms.

The most frequently reported complaint across all corridors was illusion, which in this study refers to transient sensations of dizziness, spatial disorientation, or visual discomfort, rather than optical hallucinations or neurological disorders. Such symptoms are commonly reported in environments characterized by sustained high-intensity noise and low-frequency ground vibrations, particularly during activities such as piling, tunnelling, and operation of heavy construction machinery. Prolonged exposure to low-frequency vibration is known to influence vestibular balance and concentration, consistent with observations reported in environmental noise and vibration literature31.

Breathing-related discomfort was the next most prevalent symptom, especially along Corridor 3 A, where air-quality monitoring indicated comparatively higher concentrations of PM2.5 and PM10 during dry-season construction phases. This spatial correspondence suggests an association between dust-laden air from excavation activities and respiratory irritation, as documented in earlier studies on particulate exposure32,33. Eye irritation, reported by approximately 10–15% of respondents across corridors, is plausibly linked to alkaline construction dust from cement and concrete, which can cause conjunctival dryness and irritation under dry and windy conditions.

Headaches, reported by around 10–25% of respondents, likely reflect combined exposure to particulate matter, elevated noise levels, and physical fatigue, while insomnia—though less frequently reported—suggests disruption of normal sleep patterns due to sustained background noise or extended construction activity, consistent with findings from metro construction zones elsewhere34.

caption.p: Associated health problems in the various corridors.

sec.p: Overall, the spatial distribution of reported symptoms indicates that health complaints cluster according to dominant environmental stressors—respiratory and ocular symptoms near high-dust locations, and perceptual or sleep-related discomforts near high-noise zones. These findings reinforce earlier air-quality and noise measurements presented in this study, demonstrating that metro construction impacts extend beyond environmental metrics to perceived well-being of exposed communities. While these outcomes are interpreted as associations rather than causal effects, they highlight the importance of integrating environmental monitoring with community-based health surveillance. Incorporating enhanced dust-suppression measures, regulated work hours, acoustic barriers, and transparent communication into Environmental Management Plans can reduce short-term discomfort and improve public acceptance of large-scale urban transport projects.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The study on environmental impacts of the Chennai Metro construction reveals that while metro rail expansion supports long-term sustainable mobility, its construction phase imposes notable short-term environmental stress. Air-quality analysis revealed clear seasonal and spatial variability in PM2.5 and PM10 concentrations during excavation and foundation works, with statistically significant seasonal differences indicating higher particulate levels during the pre-monsoon period. Noise levels frequently approached or exceeded CPCB standards, particularly near dense residential and traffic zones, indicating substantial acoustic disturbance during active construction periods. Spatial mapping identified localized hotspots where air and noise pollution overlapped, emphasizing the need for site-specific and phase-based mitigation. Public perception and health-survey data indicated associations between reported symptoms such as respiratory irritation, headaches, and sleep disturbance and areas experiencing elevated air and noise exposure. The findings affirm that construction-related degradation is transient and manageable through appropriate controls—dust suppression, acoustic barriers, equipment maintenance, regulated work hours, and transparent environmental monitoring. An adaptive, evidence-based management framework integrating engineering, operational, and policy measures can effectively minimize impacts while maintaining project efficiency. Overall, the research underscores that embedding sustainability into every stage of urban infrastructure development—through continuous monitoring and community participation—can transform metro projects from short-term environmental burdens into catalysts of long-term urban resilience and cleaner mobility.

sec.title: Limitations of the study
sec.p: This study did not include direct pre-construction baseline monitoring; therefore, interpretations are based on seasonal and spatial comparisons during active construction phases. Differences in air quality monitoring instruments across seasons may introduce minor variability, although consistency in observed trends was maintained. Health impacts were assessed using self-reported survey responses and are interpreted as associations rather than causal outcomes. Despite these limitations, the study provides robust evidence on construction-phase environmental dynamics relevant for urban infrastructure planning.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: PPS thanks the Management, Department of Civil Engineering and Environmental Engineering Laboratory of SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur for their invaluable support and assistance during project.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: P.P.S. and J.R.D. conceptualized the study. P.P.S., S.J.S., and P.P. were involved in methodology development, field investigation, data collection, and preliminary data compilation. A.D. led the data analysis, statistical evaluation, visualization, and interpretation of results, and was primarily responsible for revising the manuscript in response to reviewer and editor comments. A.D. also coordinated manuscript restructuring, integration of results, and finalization of the revised submission. S.B.C. and L.V. contributed to critical review, revision, and final approval of the manuscript. All authors reviewed and approved the final version of the manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: Open access funding provided by Siksha 'O' Anusandhan (Deemed To Be University). This research received no external funding.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The data supporting this study will be made available upon reasonable request from the corresponding author.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

notes.title: Consent to participate and publish
notes.p: All the authors have approved the manuscript and agree to its submission to “Scientific Reports”.

element-citation.article-title: Transportation infrastructure: Spillover effects of transport infrastructure and land value capture in Mumbai Metropolitan Region
